key_points: The essay is written by Helen Keller, who was both blind and deaf from a very young age
She describes how she experiences the world using only her sense of touch and smell
The main idea is that people with sight often 'see' very little because they take it for granted
Helen imagines what she would do if she were given the gift of sight for just three days
The story encourages readers to appreciate their senses as if they might lose them tomorrow
examples: Helen feels the 'delicate symmetry' of a leaf and the 'rough bark' of a pine tree to know nature
On Day 1, she wants to see her kind friends and the simple things in her home
On Day 2, she wants to see the sunrise and visit museums to see the history of the world
On Day 3, she wants to stand on a busy city corner and watch everyday people working and living
She calls the eyes the 'window of the soul' because they show a person's true feelings
formulas_or_processes: Day 1 → Focus on People: Seeing loved ones and friends who made her life happy
Day 2 → Focus on History & Nature: Watching the dawn and visiting museums
Day 3 → Focus on Present Life: Observing the work and daily struggle of people in the city
Moral → Use your eyes as if tomorrow you would be stricken blind
exam_tips: Remember the author's name: Helen Keller
Memorize the three-day plan (People, History/Nature, Daily Life) as it is a common question
Understand the 'Window of the Soul' metaphor referring to the eyes
Focus on the lesson of gratitude: valuing what we have before it is gone
